q: The Delhi Sultanate was founded in which year by which ruler?
explanation: The Delhi Sultanate was founded in 1206 CE by Qutbuddin Aibak, a former slave (ghulam) of Muhammad Ghori. After Ghori's death (1206), Aibak declared independence and established the Slave Dynasty (Mamluk Dynasty) in Delhi. He ruled from 1206-1210. Aibak started the construction of the Qutub Minar (completed by Iltutmish). He was known as 'Lakh Baksh' (giver of lakhs) for his generosity.

q: The Delhi Sultanate consisted of how many dynasties?
explanation: The Delhi Sultanate (1206-1526 CE) consisted of five dynasties: (1) Slave/Mamluk Dynasty (1206-1290); (2) Khalji Dynasty (1290-1320); (3) Tughlaq Dynasty (1320-1414); (4) Sayyid Dynasty (1414-1451); (5) Lodi Dynasty (1451-1526). The Sultanate lasted for 320 years until it was overthrown by Babur (Mughal) in the First Battle of Panipat (1526 CE).

q: Who was the first ruler of the Delhi Sultanate to assume the title of 'Sultan'?
explanation: Iltutmish (1211-1236 CE) was the first ruler of the Delhi Sultanate to assume the title of 'Sultan' — he made the Sultanate independent of the Caliphate's theoretical suzerainty. Iltutmish also: (1) completed the Qutub Minar (1231-1232); (2) introduced the Iqta system (land revenue assignments); (3) introduced the silver tanka and copper jital (coins); (4) defeated the Mongols. He was the real consolidator of the Delhi Sultanate.

q: The Qutub Minar in Delhi was started by Qutbuddin Aibak and completed by which ruler?
explanation: The Qutub Minar was started by Qutbuddin Aibak (1199) and completed by Iltutmish (1231-1232 CE). It is a 73m (239 ft) victory tower in Delhi, built to commemorate the victory over the Rajputs. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1993). The tower is made of red sandstone and marble, with intricate carvings of Quranic verses. Alauddin Khalji started building a second (unfinished) tower (Alai Minar) nearby.

q: Razia Sultan (1236-1240 CE) was the first woman ruler of India. She belonged to which dynasty?
explanation: Razia Sultan (Raziya al-Din, 1236-1240 CE) was the first woman ruler of India and the only woman ruler of the Delhi Sultanate. She belonged to the Slave/Mamluk Dynasty. She was the daughter of Iltutmish, who appointed her as his successor (over his sons). Razia ruled for 3.6 years — she was a capable ruler but faced opposition from conservative nobles who objected to a woman ruler. She was killed in 1240 by her own nobles. She appointed an Abyssinian (African) officer, Jamal-ud-Din Yaqut, as her close advisor.

q: Alauddin Khalji (1296-1316 CE) is famous for which military achievement?
options: Conquering most of India, including the Deccan
explanation: Alauddin Khalji (1296-1316 CE) was the most powerful ruler of the Khalji Dynasty. He conquered most of India, including: (1) Gujarat (1299); (2) Ranthambore (1301); (3) Chittorgarh (1303, where Padmini jauhar occurred); (4) Malwa (1305); (5) the Deccan (1307-1313, under his general Malik Kafur — the first penetration of the Deccan by a Delhi Sultan). He also defeated the Mongols six times — including the famous Battle of Amroha (1305). He introduced market reforms and price controls in Delhi.

q: Who was the first Delhi Sultan to successfully penetrate the Deccan?
explanation: Alauddin Khalji was the first Delhi Sultan to successfully penetrate the Deccan. His general, Malik Kafur, led expeditions to the Deccan (1307-1313) — conquering Devagiri (Yadavas of Seuna), Warangal (Kakatiyas), Dwarasamudra (Hoysalas), and Madurai (Pandyas). The Deccan kingdoms became feudatories of the Delhi Sultanate — paying tribute. Malik Kafur was a Hindu (from Gujarat) who was captured, converted to Islam, and rose to become Alauddin's most trusted general.

q: Muhammad bin Tughlaq (1325-1351 CE) is known for which failed administrative experiment?
options: Transfer of capital from Delhi to Daulatabad
explanation: Muhammad bin Tughlaq (1325-1351 CE) is known for several failed administrative experiments: (1) Transfer of capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (Devagiri, 1327) — caused massive suffering and was reversed; (2) Token currency (copper/bronze coins with the value of silver) — failed due to forgery; (3) Enhancement of taxation in the Doab — led to peasant rebellion; (4) Khurasan expedition (planning to conquer Persia) — abandoned. He was a scholar-king (learned in philosophy, mathematics, medicine) but his experiments failed due to poor execution. Known as the 'mad genius' of Indian history.

q: Firoz Shah Tughlaq (1351-1388 CE) is known for which constructive achievement?
options: Public works: canals, mosques, cities, and hospitals
explanation: Firoz Shah Tughlaq (1351-1388 CE) was a constructive ruler known for public works: (1) Canals — built four major canals including the Yamuna canal; (2) Mosques — built the Begumpuri Mosque and Firoz Shah Kotla mosque; (3) Cities — founded Firozabad, Hissar, Jaunpur, Fathabad; (4) Hospitals — established darul-shafas (hospitals); (5) Revenue reforms — introduced the rotation of crops and abolished torture. He also translated many Sanskrit works into Persian. His reign was relatively peaceful — he avoided war and focused on welfare. However, he also reinstated the jaziya tax on non-Muslims.

q: The Delhi Sultanate was brought to an end by which event?
options: First Battle of Panipat (1526 CE) — Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi
explanation: The Delhi Sultanate was brought to an end by the First Battle of Panipat (1526 CE) — Babur (the first Mughal emperor) defeated Ibrahim Lodi (the last Lodi Sultan). Babur's artillery gave him a decisive advantage. This battle marked the beginning of the Mughal Empire in India (1526-1857). The Delhi Sultanate lasted for 320 years (1206-1526). Ibrahim Lodi was killed in the battle.

q: Arrange the five dynasties of the Delhi Sultanate in chronological order: 1. Slave 2. Khalji 3. Tughlaq 4. Sayyid 5. Lodi
explanation: Slave/Mamluk (1206-1290) → Khalji (1290-1320) → Tughlaq (1320-1414) → Sayyid (1414-1451) → Lodi (1451-1526). The Slave Dynasty was founded by Qutbuddin Aibak; Khalji by Jalaluddin Khalji; Tughlaq by Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq; Sayyid by Khizr Khan; Lodi by Bahlul Lodi. The Lodi Dynasty was the only Afghan dynasty; the others were Turkish.

q: Who was the last ruler of the Lodi Dynasty (and the Delhi Sultanate)?
explanation: Ibrahim Lodi (1517-1526 CE) was the last ruler of the Lodi Dynasty and the Delhi Sultanate. He was defeated and killed by Babur at the First Battle of Panipat (1526 CE) — marking the end of the Delhi Sultanate and the beginning of the Mughal Empire. Ibrahim Lodi was an arrogant ruler who alienated his Afghan nobles — many of them invited Babur to invade. His father Sikandar Lodi (1489-1517) was a capable ruler who founded the city of Agra (1504).

q: The Iqta system of the Delhi Sultanate was:
options: A system of land revenue assignments to military commanders
A system of taxation on Hindus
explanation: The Iqta system was a system of land revenue assignments (iqtas) to military commanders (iqtadars), who were responsible for: (1) collecting revenue from their iqtas; (2) maintaining troops for the Sultan; (3) maintaining law and order. The iqtas were transferable (not hereditary) — the Sultan could transfer or recall them. The system was introduced by Iltutmish and perfected by Balban and Alauddin Khalji. It was the backbone of the Delhi Sultanate's military and revenue system.

q: Who introduced the 'token currency' (copper coins valued as silver) in the Delhi Sultanate?
explanation: Muhammad bin Tughlaq (1325-1351 CE) introduced token currency — copper and bronze coins stamped with the value of silver tankas. The experiment failed because: (1) people began forging copper coins (no security features); (2) the Sultan could not prevent forgery; (3) foreign merchants refused to accept the copper coins. The Sultan had to withdraw the token currency and repay the people in silver — causing a huge financial loss. This was one of his most famous failed experiments.

q: Alauddin Khalji's market reforms included:
options: Price controls on all essential commodities
explanation: Alauddin Khalji (1296-1316) introduced strict market reforms in Delhi: (1) price controls on all essential commodities (grain, cloth, sugar, oil, meat); (2) fixed prices — monitored by inspectors (shurta); (3) rationing — each family was given a ration card; (4) storage of grain in royal granaries; (5) punishment for hoarding and profiteering. The reforms ensured that the army was well-supplied and that the common people did not suffer from inflation. Alauddin's market reforms were one of the most ambitious economic experiments in medieval India.

q: The Mongol invasions of India during the Delhi Sultanate were successfully resisted by which ruler?
options: Alauddin Khalji (1296-1316) — defeated the Mongols six times
explanation: Alauddin Khalji (1296-1316) successfully resisted the Mongol invasions — he defeated the Mongols six times. The most important Mongol invasions were: (1) 1297 (Jaisalmer); (2) 1299 (Delhi, under Qutlugh Khwaja); (3) 1305 (Amroha — Zafar Khan defeated the Mongols). Alauddin built a strong army and a series of forts along the northwest frontier to check Mongol incursions. He was the only Delhi Sultan to decisively defeat the Mongols — other Sultans either paid tribute or fled.

q: The Slave Dynasty of the Delhi Sultanate included which famous ruler known as 'Lakh Baksh' (giver of lakhs)?
explanation: Qutbuddin Aibak (1206-1210) was known as 'Lakh Baksh' (giver of lakhs) — because of his generosity in distributing wealth. He was a former slave of Muhammad Ghori who became the first Sultan of Delhi. He started the construction of the Qutub Minar (1199) and built the Quwwat-ul-Islam Mosque (Delhi's first mosque). He died in 1210 from a polo accident. He ruled for only 4 years but laid the foundation of the Delhi Sultanate.

q: Balban (1266-1287 CE) is known for which theory of kingship?
options: The theory of 'divine right' — the Sultan is God's shadow on earth
explanation: Balban (1266-1287 CE) introduced the theory of 'divine right' — the Sultan is God's shadow (zillullah) on earth, answerable only to God. Balban also: (1) introduced the sijda (prostration) and paibos (kissing the Sultan's feet) in court; (2) maintained a magnificent court; (3) emphasised Persian court culture; (4) reorganised the army; (5) suppressed the 'Group of Forty' (Chihalgani — Turkish nobles who had become too powerful). Balban's theory of kingship was aimed at strengthening the Sultan's authority against the powerful nobles.

q: Bihar's connection with the Delhi Sultanate was through:
options: Bakhtiyar Khalji's conquest of Bihar (~1200 CE) — destroyed Nalanda and Vikramashila universities
explanation: Bihar's connection with the Delhi Sultanate was through Bakhtiyar Khalji — a Turkish general who conquered Bihar and Bengal (~1200 CE). He destroyed the famous Nalanda University and Vikramashila University (~1202 CE) — marking the end of Buddhist learning in India. The destruction of Nalanda was a catastrophic event — the library burned for months. Bakhtiyar Khalji then conquered Bengal and established the independent Khalji Dynasty of Bengal (1202-1227). Bihar and Bengal remained under Muslim rule until independence (1947).

q: The Sayyid Dynasty (1414-1451) claimed descent from which religious figure?
explanation: The Sayyid Dynasty (1414-1451 CE) claimed descent from Prophet Muhammad (through his daughter Fatima and her husband Ali — hence 'Sayyid'). The dynasty was founded by Khizr Khan (1414-1421), who was appointed governor of Multan by Timur (who sacked Delhi in 1398). The Sayyids were relatively weak — they controlled only Delhi and the surrounding area. The dynasty lasted for only 37 years before being replaced by the Lodi Dynasty. Khizr Khan's successors were: Mubarak Shah (1421-1434), Muhammad Shah (1434-1445), Alam Shah (1445-1451).

q: Timur (Tamerlane) sacked Delhi in which year, causing massive destruction?
explanation: Timur (Tamerlane, the Mongol-Turkic ruler of Samarkand) sacked Delhi in 1398 CE — causing massive destruction. He invaded India during the reign of the Tughlaq Sultan Nasiruddin Mahmud (1394-1412). Timur's army looted Delhi for three days, killed thousands of people, and destroyed many buildings. The sack of Delhi (1398) weakened the Delhi Sultanate severely — it led to the decline of the Tughlaq Dynasty and the rise of the Sayyid Dynasty (1414). Timur took with him a vast amount of treasure and skilled artisans to Samarkand.

q: Who founded the city of Agra in 1504?
explanation: Sikandar Lodi (1489-1517 CE) founded the city of Agra in 1504 — he shifted his capital from Delhi to Agra. Sikandar Lodi was a capable ruler of the Lodi Dynasty — he also conquered Bihar (1490s) and improved administration. He was a patron of learning — he wrote poetry in Persian under the pen name 'Gulruk.' His son Ibrahim Lodi (1517-1526) was the last Lodi Sultan — defeated by Babur at Panipat (1526).

q: Which Delhi Sultan shifted the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (Devagiri)?
explanation: Muhammad bin Tughlaq (1325-1351) shifted the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (Devagiri, in present-day Maharashtra) in 1327 CE. His idea was to have a more central capital (Delhi was too far north to control the Deccan). He ordered the entire population of Delhi to move to Daulatabad — causing massive suffering and deaths on the journey. After a few years, he realised the mistake and shifted the capital back to Delhi. Daulatabad was abandoned. This was one of his most famous failed experiments.

q: The Chihalgani (Group of Forty) was a powerful group of which type of people in the Delhi Sultanate?
options: Turkish slave nobles who dominated the early Sultanate
explanation: The Chihalgani (Group of Forty) was a powerful group of 40 Turkish slave nobles who dominated the early Delhi Sultanate (Slave Dynasty). They were originally the slaves of Iltutmish — he trained them as administrators and military commanders. After Iltutmish's death, the Chihalgani became kingmakers — they installed and deposed Sultans at will. They were finally suppressed by Balban (1266-1287), who saw them as a threat to the Sultan's authority. Balban had many of the Chihalgani killed or exiled — ending their dominance.

q: Alauddin Khalji conquered Chittorgarh in 1303 CE. This event is associated with which legend?
explanation: Alauddin Khalji conquered Chittorgarh in 1303 CE. This event is associated with the legend of Padmini — the queen of Chittorgarh. According to the legend (recorded in Malik Muhammad Jayasi's Padmavat, 1540), Alauddin invaded Chittorgarh to obtain Padmini. When the Rajputs were defeated, Padmini and other Rajput women committed jauhar (self-immolation) to avoid capture. The historical accuracy of the Padmini legend is debated, but the jauhar of Chittorgarh (1303) is historical. This was the first of three jauhars at Chittorgarh.
